From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Cyrillic Romanization system

"Transliteration of Cyrillic" redirects here. For the broader topic of all forms of romanization of Cyrillic, see [Romanization of Cyrillic](/wiki/Romanization_of_Cyrillic_(disambiguation) "Romanization of Cyrillic (disambiguation)").

**Scientific transliteration of Cyrillic** is an international method for [transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") of text from the [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") to the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") ([romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization")). This system is most often seen in [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics") publications on [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages").

Scientific transliteration, also called *academic*, *linguistic*, *international*, or *scholarly transliteration*, was first introduced in 1898 as part of the standardization process for the *[Preußische Instruktionen](/wiki/Preu%C3%9Fische_Instruktionen "Preußische Instruktionen")* (PI; a library cataloging system used in German-speaking countries) in 1899. Despite the name, "scientific transliteration" has no connection to the [scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method") or modern science[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Scientific_transliteration_of_Cyrillic#Dubious "Talk:Scientific transliteration of Cyrillic")*]; the word *science* before the 20th century usually referred to [knowledge](/wiki/Knowledge "Knowledge") in general, rather than referring to [natural](/wiki/Natural_science "Natural science") or [applied sciences](/wiki/Applied_science "Applied science") in specific.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Details

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scientific_transliteration_of_Cyrillic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Details")]

The scientific transliteration system is roughly as [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic "Phonemic") as is the orthography of the language transliterated. The deviations are with щ where the transliteration makes clear that two phonemes are involved, and џ, where it fails to represent the (monophonemic) affricate with a single letter. The transliteration system is based on the [Gaj's Latin alphabet](/wiki/Gaj%27s_Latin_alphabet "Gaj's Latin alphabet") used in [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian_language "Serbo-Croatian language"), in which each letter corresponds directly to a Cyrillic letter in [Bosnian](/wiki/Bosnian_language "Bosnian language"), [Montenegrin](/wiki/Montenegrin_language "Montenegrin language") and [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language") official standards, and was heavily based on the earlier [Czech alphabet](/wiki/Czech_alphabet "Czech alphabet"). The Cyrillic letter х, representing the sound [x] as in *Bach*, was romanized *h* in Serbo-Croatian, but in German-speaking countries the native digraph *ch* was used instead.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was codified in the 1898 *Prussian Instructions* for libraries, or *Preußische Instruktionen* (PI), which were adopted in Central Europe and Scandinavia. Scientific transliteration can also be used to romanize the early [Glagolitic alphabet](/wiki/Glagolitic_alphabet "Glagolitic alphabet"), which has a close correspondence to Cyrillic.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Scientific transliteration is often adapted to serve as a phonetic alphabet.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Scientific transliteration was the basis for the [ISO 9](/wiki/ISO_9 "ISO 9") transliteration standard. While linguistic transliteration tries to preserve the original language's [pronunciation](/wiki/Pronunciation "Pronunciation") to a certain degree, the latest version of the ISO standard (ISO 9:1995) has abandoned this concept, which was still found in [ISO/R 9:1968](/wiki/ISO/R_9:1968 "ISO/R 9:1968") and is now restricted to a one-to-one mapping of letters. It thus allows for unambiguous reverse transliteration into the original Cyrillic text and is language-independent.

The previous official Soviet romanization system, [GOST 16876-71](/wiki/GOST_16876-71 "GOST 16876-71"), is also based on scientific transliteration but used Latin *h* for Cyrillic х instead of Latin *x* or *ssh* and *sth* for Cyrillic Щ, and had a number of other differences. Most countries using Cyrillic script now have adopted [GOST 7.79](/wiki/GOST_7.79 "GOST 7.79") instead, which is not the same as ISO 9 but close to it.

Representing all of the necessary diacritics on computers requires [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode"), [Latin-2](/wiki/Latin-2 "Latin-2"), [Latin-4](/wiki/Latin-4 "Latin-4"), or [Latin-7](/wiki/Latin-7 "Latin-7") encoding.

( ) Letters in parentheses are older or alternative transliterations. Ukrainian and Belarusian apostrophe are not transcribed. The early Cyrillic letter [koppa](/wiki/Koppa_(Cyrillic) "Koppa (Cyrillic)") (Ҁ, ҁ) was used only for transliterating Greek and its [numeric value](/wiki/Cyrillic_numerals "Cyrillic numerals") and was thus omitted. Prussian Instructions and ISO 9:1995 are provided for comparison.

Unicode encoding is:

* U+02BC ʼ MODIFIER LETTER APOSTROPHE for the Cyrillic apostrophe
* U+02B9 ʹ MODIFIER LETTER PRIME to transliterate the soft sign[[5]](#cite_note-prime-7)
* U+02BA ʺ MODIFIER LETTER DOUBLE PRIME to transliterate the hard sign[[5]](#cite_note-prime-7)
